title: Amelia Henry
text:
Welcome
This site is essentially a living lab notebook; a running collection of how I work with data over time.
It brings together my approaches, projects, and things I have learned along the way. Everything here is built openly and documented as I go.
All data used here is simulated or publicly available, and the focus is on my approach and processes to conducting rigorous, transparent, and reproducible research, rather than on particular findings.
I like to think of this as both a portfolio, and a working record.
For prospective collaborators and employers, this serves as a transparent record of how I think through analytical problems and make decisions, from framing research questions, to choosing methods of analysis, to conducting/interpreting and communicating results.
For students and early career researchers, this can potentially serve as a learning resource. Most posts include comments/annotations outlining what I did, but more importantly why.
If you find something useful, have any questions, notice something is off/spotted an error, or just want to chat, feel free to reach out! You can find me on LinkedIn or email.

title: Project Setup
text: purpose

title: Project Setup
text:
project/

├── R/
├── data/
│   ├── raw/
│   ├── versions/
│   └── final/
├── outputs/
├── docs/
├── renv.lock

title: Project Setup
text:
File paths

Always anchor paths to project root
Avoid using absolute paths, always use relative paths


# use here::here() to construct paths 
here::i_am("R/01_data_processing.R")

# use relative paths 
df_raw <- readr::read_csv(here::here("data/raw/raw_survey.csv"))

title: Project Setup
text:
Project environment

Initialise once, on first open
This is useful for reproducibility and sharing with collaborators


# create renv environment and lockfile in root folder
renv::init()
# install packages relevant to the project 
install.packages(c("tidyverse", "here", "renv", "usethis", "devtools"))

# snapshot to save in lockfile for reproducibility and collaboration
renv::snapshot()

notes: - renv::renv.lock defines the environment state of the project - collaborators can use renv::restore() to restore the same environment on their machine

title: Project Setup
text:
Secret/private info management

Use usethis to create .Renviron file in root folder and add secrets there (e.g., API keys, database credentials)
This file is ignored by Git/GitHub and is not shared with collaborators, so it is a safe place to store sensitive information.


# create .Renviron file in root folder and add secrets there
usethis::edit_r_environ()

This will open the .Renviron file, and you need to enter the info in the following format:
DB_USERNAME="your_db_username_here"
DB_PASSWORD="your_db_password_here"
After saving the .Renviron file, you can access the secrets in your R code:

api_key <- Sys.getenv("API_KEY")
db_username <- Sys.getenv("DB_USERNAME")
db_password <- Sys.getenv("DB_PASSWORD")

title: Project Setup
text:
keep everything organised
reproducible and transparent structure
clear workflow/file organisation

title: Project Setup
text:
structure

create project root folder
open in software

project/

├── R/
├── data/
│   ├── raw/        # source data (never edited)
│   ├── versions/   # timestamped snapshots
│   ├── processed/  # cleaned / merged
│   └── final/      # datasets used for analysis / sharing
├── outputs/        # figures, tables, model objects
├── docs/           # protocols, codebooks, notes
├── renv.lock
notes: - root folder always treated as root - no setwd() - all paths relative - never change raw data

title: Project Setup
text:
environment
initialise on first open:

# create renv and lockfile in root folder 
renv::init()

# install packages 
install.packages(c("tidyverse", "here", "renv", "usethis", "devtools"))

# snapshot to save in lockfile for reproducibility and collaboration
renv::snapshot()

notes: - renv.lockfile tracks package versions - collaborators use renv::restore() to get same environment

paths

# import data using relative paths
readr::read_csv(here::here("data/raw/survey_data_raw.csv"))

title: My Coding Practices
text: Purpose:

title: My Coding Practices
text:
Structure projects properly for reproducibility and transparency
Use tools to make workflow easier and more efficient (e.g., R projects, renv, Git/GitHub)
Document my workflow and approaches for others to learn from and potentially re-use

title: My Coding Practices
text:
Project set up
Usually, I create project root folder and open in Positron (or RStudio as new project)
project-root/
├── R/              # this is where all R scripts and functions go 
├── data/
│   ├── raw/        # treat as ready only - never edit these files
│   ├── versions/   # any edits to data should be saved here with version number and date (e.g., survey_data_v1_2024-06-01.csv)
│   └── final/    # this is where the final data files that are used for analyses/collaboration go (e.g., survey_data_final.csv)
├── outputs/     # this is where all saved outputs go (e.g., tables, figures, model objects). 
├── docs/     # this is where all documentation useful for analyses goes (e.g., project proposal, data dictionary, codebook, guides, protocols etc)
├── renv.lock # this is where the renv lockfile goes (after running renv::snapshot()) which is useful for reproducibility and sharing with collaborators
Some rules I like to follow:

treat root as the root folder always (never use set_wd() or absolute paths).
use relative paths from root (e.g., data/raw/survey_data.csv) and use here::here() to construct paths in code (e.g., here::here("data/raw/survey_data.csv"))
data/raw/ is read only - never edit these files.


Set up environment (just once)

# create renv environment and lockfile in root folder 
# this is useful for reproducibility and sharing with collaborators
renv::init() 

# install packages that will always be used in the project (e.g., tidyverse, here, etc) 
# for example: 
install.packages(c("tidyverse", "here", "renv", "usethis", "devtools"))

# and then snapshot to save in lockfile to  ensure reproducibility and sharing with collaborators
renv::snapshot()

title: My Coding Practices
text:
Lock in secrets
Sometimes there may be sensitive information that is needed for analyses (e.g., API keys, database credentials).
I like to use the usethis package to create a .Renviron file in the root folder and add any secrets there. This file is ignored by Git/GitHub and is not shared with collaborators, so it is a safe place to store sensitive information.

# create .Renviron file in root folder and add secrets there 
# (e.g., API keys, database credentials)
usethis::edit_r_environ()

This will open the .Renviron file, and you need to enter the info in the following format:
    API_KEY="your_api_key_here"
    DB_USERNAME="your_db_username_here"
    DB_PASSWORD="your_db_password_here"
After saving the .Renviron file, you can access the secrets in your R code using Sys.getenv() function. For example:

api_key <- Sys.getenv("API_KEY")
db_username <- Sys.getenv("DB_USERNAME")
db_password <- Sys.getenv("DB_PASSWORD")

This way, you can keep your sensitive information secure while still being able to use it in your analyses

title: My Coding Practices
text:
Script organisation
I start every script with a header that includes the title of the script, the date, and a brief description of what the script does. This helps me and others understand the purpose of the script at a glance.
For example:

# 01_data_processing.R
# Date: 2024-06-01
# Description: This script is for cleaning and processing the raw data from the survey.

I also like to organise my scripts based on the purpose of the script. For example, I might have separate scripts for data processing, running linear models, and utility functions. I will make that clear in the script name and the header of the script, and I will organise the scripts in the R/ folder based on their purpose.
For example:
    R/
    ├── 01_data_processing.R   # scripts for cleaning and processing data
    ├── 02_linear_models.R     # scripts for running linear models
    ├── utilities.R           # scripts for functions that are used across multiple analyses
    ├── functions_data_processing.R  # scripts for functions that are specific to data processing

title: My Coding Practices
My Coding Practices
text:
Sourcing and Targeting
If I have multiple scripts, I introduce targets to make it clear which script is the main script that should be run to execute the entire workflow.
For example, I might have a main.R script that sources the other scripts in the correct order, and I will make it clear in the header of the main.R script that this is the main script that should be run.
I introduce targets after: - we have multiple scripts (e.g., data processing, running linear models, utility functions) - we have a clear order in which the scripts should be run (e.g., data processing should be run before running linear models) - we want to make it clear to collaborators which script is the main script that should be run to execute the entire workflow.
To do so, I create a main.R script that sources the other scripts in the correct order. For example:

# inside the main.r script:
list(
    tar_target(raw, get_data()),
    tar_target(cleaned, clean_data(raw)),
    tar_target(model, fit_linear_model(cleaned)),
    tar_target(plot, create_plot(model))
)

Ultimately this helps to make the workflow more organised and easier to understand. It also: - Avoids manually re-running scripts in the correct order, which can be error-prone and time-consuming - Helps to track the dependencies between scripts and ensures that the workflow is executed in the correct order

File paths
I always use the here::here() function to construct file paths in my scripts. For example:

here::i_am("R/01_data_processing.R")  # this tells here that the root folder is the one that contains the R folder

By doing this, I can ensure that all file paths in my scripts are relative to the root folder, regardless of where the script is located; which makes my code more portable and easier to share with collaborators.
It also makes importing or saving files easier, as I can use relative paths from the root folder without worrying about the current working directory.
For example, instead of using an absolute path like this:

data <- read_csv("/Users/username/project-root/data/raw/survey_data.csv")

I would use:

data <- read_csv(here::here("data/raw/survey_data.csv"))

This way, I can run the script on any computer without having to change the file paths, as long as the project structure is the same.
Note: If you send your collaborators your root folder, they can run the script without having to change any file paths, as long as they do not change the project structure.

title: My Coding Practices
text:
Naming and structure conventions
I try to follow consistent naming conventions for my files, functions, and variables. This makes it easier to understand the purpose of each file, function, and variable at a glance. In practice, this looks like:

snake_case everywhere: e.g., `psychological_distress”, “alcohol_consumption_10_years”
verb-based functions: e.g., clean_data(), fit_linear_model(), create_plot()
descriptive data object: e.g., df_survey_adolescent,model_summary_psychological_distress

title: Script Practices
text:
organisation
Example layout of script folder:

title: Script Practices
text:
one script = one purpose
avoid combining stages of large analyses
keep scripts short


R/
├── 01_data_processing.R   # scripts for cleaning and processing data
├── 02_linear_models.R     # scripts for running linear models 
├── utilities.R           # scripts for functions that are used across multiple analyses
├── functions_data_processing.R  # scripts for functions that are specific to data processing

title: Script Practices
text:
functions

any repeated logic gets moved to functions
if repeated across different tasks -> utilities.R
if task-specific -> functions_01_data_cleaning.R
source those scripts when needed in analyses

title: Script Practices
Script Practices
text:
naming conventions

use snake_case for everything
clear descriptive names (not df1)
objects start with noun (e.g., df_* or mod_*)
functions start with verb (e.g., clean_, fit_)

title: Script Practices
text:
ordering and flow

if structure begins to exist (e.g., order of scripts) use targets
removes manual ordering and execution
tracks dependencies


list(
  tar_target(raw, get_data()),
  tar_target(cleaned, clean_data(raw)),
  tar_target(model, fit_linear_model(cleaned)),
  tar_target(plot, create_plot(model))
)

title: About Me
text:
I am a Postdoctoral Research Fellow at the University of Sydney, where I am part of the Matilda Centre for Research in Mental Health and Substance Use. My research sits at the intersection of Social Psychology and Epidemiology, with a particular focus on social identity and intergroup relations in relation to mental health, wellbeing, and co-occurring risk behaviours (e.g., alcohol use) and conditions. Currently, I am interested in co-occurring psychological distress and alcohol use problems in adults with ADHD, as well as how ADHD-related relationships and networks may shape these patterns.
I received my PhD in Social Psychology from Western Sydney University in 2024, completed under the supervision of Professor Craig McGarty and Professor Nida Denson. My PhD examined how advantaged groups strategically use prejudice expression to undermine or demobilise collective action by disadvantaged groups. Across this work, I focused on how people maintain social hierarchies when their group identity is challenged or threatened, and how strategies typically framed as identity management can also function as forms of prejudice expression. This involved a series of studies, including large-scale online surveys, experimental designs, and computational analyses of social media data.
In 2022, I joined the Matilda Centre as a Project Coordinator, where I led the development, implementation, and evaluation of Stable Ground, a digital stepped-care intervention for alcohol and other drug use among Australian public safety personnel. Since then, I have worked on a series of epidemiological projects exploring co-occurring psychological distress and alcohol use problems across different groups in Australia, and am currently pursuing the development of my own research program on these comorbidities among adults with ADHD.

title: Negative Binomial Hurdle Models with Zero-Heavy Count Data
text:
Modelling Alcohol and Other Drug Use, Psychological Distress, and Occupational Functioning in Australian Public Safety Personnel
(full script at bottom of page to come)
crumbs: Negative Binomial Hurdle Models with Zero-Heavy Count Data

title: Negative Binomial Hurdle Models with Zero-Heavy Count Data
text:
Overview
Examining how alcohol and other drug use and psychological distress relate to absenteeism and presenteeism in public safety personnel.
Key Variables:

Psychological distress (K10)
Substance Use (ASSIST)
Workplace Outcomes

Outcomes:

Absenteeism and Presenteeism (from k10+)
Count data with many zeros
crumbs: Negative Binomial Hurdle Models with Zero-Heavy Count Data

title: Negative Binomial Hurdle Models with Zero-Heavy Count Data
text:
Data Pre-processing
Cleaning was mostly focused on producing an analysis-ready dataset, as data were already in usable format. See diagram below for an overview of the full process.


Option 2: Slightly more “decision-focused”





flowchart TD

A[Raw survey export] --> B[Variable inspection & renaming]
B --> C[Scale scoring]

C --> C1[K10 total score]
C --> C2[ASSIST alcohol score]

C1 --> D[Outcome preparation]
C2 --> D

D --> D1[Absenteeism (days out of role)]
D --> D2[Presenteeism (days not functioning)]

D1 --> E[Data checks]
D2 --> E

E --> F[Final dataset (df_clean)]
crumbs: Negative Binomial Hurdle Models with Zero-Heavy Count Data

title: Negative Binomial Hurdle Models with Zero-Heavy Count Data
text:
Research Question/Aims

Examine rates of severity of alcohol and other drug (AOD) use among public safety personnel (PSP)
Examine associations between AOD use, psychological distress, and occupational functioning (absenteeism and presenteeism) among PSP
Examine whether AOD use and psychological distress predict impairment to occupational function, both independently, and in combination
crumbs: Negative Binomial Hurdle Models with Zero-Heavy Count Data

title: Negative Binomial Hurdle Models with Zero-Heavy Count Data
text:
Data Pre-processing/Screening
Initial visualisation of absenteeism and presenteeism showed:

strong right-skew
large proportion of zeros


df %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Absenteeism = k10_11_absenteeism,
    Presenteeism = k10_12_presenteeism
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(Absenteeism, Presenteeism),
    names_to = "Variable",
    values_to = "Days"
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(Days)) |>
  gghistogram(
    x = "Days",
    y = "count",
    xlab = "Days",
    ylab = FALSE,
    add = "median",
    facet.by = "Variable",
    binwidth = 1
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 30, by = 5))








note: suggests standard linear models would be inappropriate. count-based better
crumbs: Negative Binomial Hurdle Models with Zero-Heavy Count Data

title: Negative Binomial Hurdle Models with Zero-Heavy Count Data
text:
Model selection
Given the distributional properties of absenteeism and presenteeism, I considered several candidate models:

Poisson regression
Negative binomial regression
Zero-inflated Poisson (ZIP)
Zero-inflated negative binomial models (ZINB)
Hurdle negative binomial models


Considerations:
Overdispersion: Poisson models assume that the mean = variance. Preliminary checks indicated overdispersion, making Poisson models unsuitable.
Excess zeros: The data contained more zeros than expected under standard count models. This raises 2 choices:

Zero inflated models: assume two latent groups (always-zero vs always at-risk)
Hurdle models: assume a two-stage process (zero vs positive counts)
crumbs: Negative Binomial Hurdle Models with Zero-Heavy Count Data

title: Negative Binomial Hurdle Models with Zero-Heavy Count Data
text:
Modelling strategy

Univariate models - all types
Here I made a function that ran a series of univariate models on each outcome to check dispersion This helped to evaluate which count model handled the outcome data the best. Univariate predictors included age, gender, years of employment, k10 total score, maximum alcohol use risk score, and maximum other drug use risk score.
Models were compared using: AIC/BIC (fit), dispersion stats, vuong tests, predicted vs observed zeros.
Although zero-inflated models and hurdle models were basically the same, MS and I discussed the choice from a conceptual angle, and we agreed that the zero-inflation was too rigid with the “always-zero” or “always at-risk” approach.
Given we are looking at absenteeism and presenteeism, days of each is unlikely to be always.
So we chose Hurdle Negative Binomial Models.

Reasons:

they count for overdispersion
explicitly model the probability of any absenteeism/presenteeism (logistic) and the frequency of absenteeism/presenteeism among those who didn’t have zero values (count)
conceptually, they distinguish between whether someone is absent and how often they are absent.
more aligned with our aims/RQs

Once Hurdle Models were chosen, each predictor was first examined in isolation to:

identify potential associations
understand variable-specific effects
identify which covariates to include in multivariable analysis



Insights:
absenteeism:
crumbs: Negative Binomial Hurdle Models with Zero-Heavy Count Data
